rationale: The cited paper is used in the theory-development section as concrete prior evidence that a high-price firm image can reduce consumers' conservation compliance. The citing authors explicitly position their work relative to it by stating that the prior study did not test social class or entitlement, then using that gap to motivate their own mechanism and hypotheses. This is more than a background list mention because it helps justify the paper's core conceptual setup around price-linked entitlement in socially costly consumption.
relation_description: Lee and Winterich (2022) build on Wang et al. (2017)'s finding that high-price contexts can undermine conservation behavior, and extend it by proposing and testing entitlement and social class as explanatory drivers in socially costly product choice.
